coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots by B/P doping and application for Co2+ and methylene blue detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunyan
Li, Nan
Yang, Liang
Liu, Lei
Zhang, Daohan
coredata.dc:description: During the synthesis of carbon dots, different heteroatom doping can change the fluorescence color and the emission wavelength when the same precursor is applied. In this study, we used o-phenylenediamine and L-lysine as raw materials, boric acid or phosphoric acid as different heteroatom dopants, and water as solvent to synthesize doped carbon dots through a simple microwave-assisted method. Finally, two kinds of doped carbon dots with different fluorescence colors under 365 nm UV light were obtained, of which the B-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) showed orange fluorescence, and the P-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) showed red fluorescence. Both carbon dots had satisfactory fluorescence quantum yields and can be used as fluorescence probes. Therefore, we investigated the detection performance of these two carbon dots when they were used as fluorescent probes. B-CDs can selectively detect Co2+ with a detection limit of 0.1102 μM, and at the same time, it can realize the naked eye detection of Co2+ in a certain concentration range. The P-CDs could sensitively detect methylene blue with a detection limit of 0.048 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Detection
B-doped
P-doped
Co2+
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective detection of MB by P-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and particle size distributions of B-CDs (a) and P-CDs (b); UV, excitation, and emission peaks of B-CDs (c) and P-CDs (d) (inset: photographs of B-CDs and P-CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamps).
Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs (a), and P-CDs (b) at different excitation wavelengths; FT-IR spectra of B-CDs (c) and P-CDs (d).
(a): XPS spectra of B-CDs; (b): high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s; (c): O1s; (d): N1s; (e): B1s.
(a): XPS spectra of P-CDs; (b): high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s; (c): N1s; (d): O1s; (e): P2p.

(a): Fluorescence profiles of B-CDs after the addition of different cations (10−3 M) at 469 nm excitation wavelength; (b): fluorescence intensity of B-CDs with different cations (10−3 M); (c) Interference of other cations in the detection of Co2+.

(a): Fluorescence profiles of B-CDs solutions at Co2+ concentrations from 0 to 50 μM; (b): Linear plots of (I0-I)/I0 versus Co2+ concentration from 1 to 30 μM; (c): UV absorption profiles of B-CDs blank solution and B-CDs with Co2+ concentrations of 4 μM and 40 μM; (d): Excitation spectra of B-CDs and Co2+ (10−2 M) UV absorption profiles.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of P-CDs after addition of different substance (10−4 M); (b) MB detection in the presence of other interferents; (c) Fluorescence profiles of P-CDs solution with addition of MB concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 60 μM; (d) Linear plots of (I0-I)/I0 versus MB concentration from 0.1 to 10 μM.

(a): UV absorption of P-CDs blank solution and P-CDs containing MB at concentrations of 6 μM and 60 μM; (b): Linear plots of MB concentration versus I0/I at different temperatures (5 °C, 25 °C, 70 °C); (c): The fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of P-CDs in the presence and absence of MB.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots by B/P doping and application for Co2+ and methylene blue detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the synthesis of carbon dots, different heteroatom doping can change the fluorescence color and the emission wavelength when the same precursor is applied. In this study, we used o-phenylenediamine and L-lysine as raw materials, boric acid or phosphoric acid as different heteroatom dopants, and water as solvent to synthesize doped carbon dots through a simple microwave-assisted method. Finally, two kinds of doped carbon dots with different fluorescence colors under 365 nm UV light were obtained, of which the B-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) showed orange fluorescence, and the P-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) showed red fluorescence. Both carbon dots had satisfactory fluorescence quantum yields and can be used as fluorescence probes. Therefore, we investigated the detection performance of these two carbon dots when they were used as fluorescent probes. B-CDs can selectively detect Co2+ with a detection limit of 0.1102 μM, and at the same time, it can realize the naked eye detection of Co2+ in a certain concentration range. The P-CDs could sensitively detect methylene blue with a detection limit of 0.048 μM.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt (Co) is an essential mineral element which plays a vital role in maintaining normal life activities as a component of vitamin B12, which is necessary for the synthesis of DNA in living organisms. However, excessive intake of Co2+ in human body may cause heart failure, asthma, cramps, diarrhea, hypotension, bone defects, gastric dysfunction, and cancer [1]. In recent years, Co2+ has become a common dopant and catalyst in the production of new materials and renewable energy sources, and is widely used in electroplating, dye, mining industries, which can cause serious environmental pollution when improperly handled [2]. Co2+ as a heavy metal ion is also considered to be a possible carcinogen, which not only affects the water quality, but also be a threat to the survival of aquatic organisms. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR) in US stipulates that: the concentration of Co2+ in the soil is between 1 ∼ 40 mg/mL, the concentration of Co2+ in the drinking water is usually less than 1 ∼ 2 ng/mL, the concentration of Co2+ in water for irrigation and livestock is usually less than 1 ∼ 2 ng/mL [3]. In Canada, Co2+ in irrigation water and livestock water are limited to maximum concentrations of 0.05 and 1.0 mg/L, respectively (Environmental Bureau of Investigation, Canadian Water Quality Guidelines) [4]. Therefore, accurate detection of Co2+ in water is crucial. There are some literatures on the use of carbon dots as fluorescent probes for Co2+ detection, e.g., Kong et al. prepared carbon dots with a quantum yield of 38.7 % from carbopol 934 and diethylenetriamine by hydrothermal heating for 5 h. The prepared carbon dots showed high sensitivity and selectivity for Co2+ with good linear response in the range of 0 ∼ 40 μM and the detection limit was 0.45 μM [5]; Shi et al. prepared green fluorescent carbon dots with a quantum yield of about 9.8 % by hydrothermal treatment of tyrosine and urea for 3 h. The detection limit of the green carbon dots for Co2+ was 0.15 μM [6].

Methylene blue (MB) is a phenothiazine salt widely used in chemical indicators, dyes, biological stains and drugs. High concentrations of MB can poison and kill animals. MB is classified as a Group III carcinogen in the list of carcinogens published by the World Health Organization in 2017. As a toxic cationic dye, MB can cause allergic dermatitis, cancer, skin irritation, and eye burns in humans and animals. It is one of the common pollutants in factories and colored waste water. In order to strictly control and monitor the illegal discharge of MB by enterprises, the detection of MB in wastewater is necessary. Currently, the main methods for the detection of MB are spectrophotometry [7], liquid chromatography [8] and capillary electrophoresis [9], which are low sensitive, or cumbersome and costly to operate, so it is very important to develop a convenient, rapid, cost-effective, easy-to-operate method for the detection of MB. In contrast, fluorescence detection has the advantages of easy operation and sensitive response. By now, there are few literatures on the use of carbon dots to detect MB. Zhao et al. utilized a red fluorescent carbon dot with a fluorescence quantum yield of 30.2 % for MB detection [10]. A one-step solvothermal method was used in the synthesis process, which was time-consuming. And the red carbon dots were separated by column chromatography to achieve the best performance, which was a cumbersome process. Xue et al. obtained a green fluorescent carbon dot with a fluorescence quantum yield of 10.6 % by pyrolysis at high temperature of 300 °C for 2 h for the detection of MB [11], which shortened the synthesis time but the quantum yield was decreased.

Carbon dots have been proved very promising for sensor applications. The analytes can be chelated with the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots or induce electron transfer to enhance or quench the fluorescence of the carbon dots to achieve detection [12]. The sensitivity and selectivity of the detection depend on the affinity between the active groups on the surface of the carbon dots and the analytes. One way to improve the detection selectivity is doping with heteroatoms to introduce reactive functional groups [13]. Meanwhile, the luminescent properties of carbon dots can also be improved by doping with nitrogen, boron, and phosphorus etc [14,15]. Most of the fluorescent carbon dots reported in the literature are blue and green luminescent [16]. For example, Shangguan et al. synthesized an N and P co-doped blue fluorescent carbon dots by hydrothermal method using ATP as a raw material [17]. Zhang et al. used glutathione (GSH) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as raw materials in aqueous solution at pH = 6 to obtain green fluorescent carbon dots [18]. The preparation of long-wavelength-emitting fluorescent carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield is still a great challenge [19]. Especially the red fluorescent carbon dots, which generally have low fluorescence quantum yields, are still attractive for researchers due to their advantages of long wave-length emission, high penetrability and versatility for biomedical, optoelectronic devices and fluorescent probes [20,21]. Scientists have made various explanations about the formation process and fluorescence mechanism of carbon dots, such as intrinsic state, surface state and molecular state [22–26]. However, regardless of the mechanism, the selection of suitable raw materials and solvents as well as the synthesis method are key to the fluorescence properties of carbon dots [27,28]. The isomers of phenylenediamine are considered to be important raw materials for the preparation of long wavelength emitting fluorescent carbon dots [29],which also introduce N, resulting in better photostability [30]. It is reported in the literature that o-phenylenediamine (OPD) is suitable to synthesize red carbon dots because the carbon core structure produced by OPD under suitable synthesis conditions can emit red light. The carbon core structure dominates the fluorescence color, and the suitable dopants can improve quantum yield of the carbon dots [31]. Some literatures have synthesized red fluorescent carbon dots from OPD and phosphoric acid [32–34]. However, they generally have low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and involve time-consuming synthesis method. According to the literature, large molecular weight dopant may affect the fluorescence QY of red carbon dots. And it has been demonstrated in the literature that L-lysine can enhance the fluorescence intensity of red fluorescent carbon dots, but does not change its emission wavelength [35].

In this study, we used OPD and L-lysine as raw materials, water as solvent, boric acid for boron doping and phosphoric acid for phosphorus doping, respectively, to obtain two fluorescent carbon dots by a simple and rapid microwave-assisted method. Boron doped carbon dots (B-CDs) emit orange fluorescence, and phosphorus doped carbon dots (P-CDs) emit red fluorescence. B-CDs showed a sensitive selectivity to Co2+, and its fluorescence could be quenched by Co2+ to realize the quantitative analysis and detection of Co2+. P-CDs showed a sensitive selectivity to methylene blue in the same way. Both carbon dots have high fluorescence quantum yields, with the relative fluorescence QY of 77.21 % for B-CDs and 46.19 % for P-CDs. By comparing B-CDs and P-CDs we can see that the fluorescence color, the emission wavelength, and the fluorescence QY of the carbon dots, as well as their detection performance, all change with different heteroatoms when other conditions are the same. In order to verify that the addition of L-lysine enhances the relative QY of the red-fluorescent carbon dots, we also synthesized red-fluorescent R-CDs from OPD and phosphoric acid using the same synthetic method, and the fluorescence QY of R-CDs and P-CDs were compared. The fluorescence QY of R-CDs was 24.38 %. It is obvious that the fluorescence QY of P-CDs is higher, which justifies the effectiveness of adding L-lysine in the synthesis process.

All purchased reagents and chemicals were used directly without further purification. The experimental water was deionized water. OPD (Macklin), L-lysine (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), phosphoric acid (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), boric acid (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), copper nitrate (Macklin), zinc chloride (Macklin), anhydrous magnesium chloride (Macklin), chromium nitrate hydrate (Macklin), potassium chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Macklin), ferrous sulfate (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Ferric Nitrate nonahydrate (Macklin), Cuprous Chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Anhydrous Aluminum Chloride (Macklin), Anhydrous Calcium Chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Methylene Blue (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Iodide (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Bromide (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Fluoride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Carbonate (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Hydrogen Carbonate (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Sodium Sulfate (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Trisodium phosphate, malachite green (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), sodium nitrite (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory).

The CDs were synthesized by using an APL microwave digestion instrument (APL Technology Group Co., Ltd.). The CDs were dried in a DZ-28C vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Taiste Instrument Co., Ltd.). The fluorescence spectrum was measured by an F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Prismatic Technology Co., Ltd.) The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by a TU-1900 double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-Beijing Pudian General Instruments Co. Ltd.), JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) was used to obtain the transmission electron microscope images of CDs. Nicolet6700 infrared Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA, at Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com)) was used to obtain the infrared spectra. and ESCALAB 250Xi x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to obtain the X-ray spectra of CDs. Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to obtain x-ray photoelectron spectra.

Preparation of B-CDs: OPD (0.1 g), L-lysine (0.15 g) and boric acid (0.15 g) were ultrasonicated for 5 min to dissolve completely in 30 mL of water. Then it was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and microwaved at 180 °C for 30 min. At the end of the reaction, it was naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain an orange liquid product. The orange liquid product was purified by 1000 Da dialysis bag for 7 h. The collected orange liquid was put into the refrigerator for further study, named as B-CDs.

Preparation of P-CDs: OPD (0.1 g), L-lysine (0.15 g) were ultrasonicated for 5 min to completely dissolve in 30 mL of water, and then 5 mL of phosphoric acid was add, the heating and purification process is the same as B-CDs. Finally, the orange solution was obtained and put in the refrigerator for further study, named as P-CDs.

Preparation of R-CDs: OPD (0.1 g) was ultrasonicated for 5 min until completely dissolved in 30 mL of water, and then 5 mL of phosphoric acid was added. The preparation and treatment methods were the same as for P-CDs. The final orange solution was placed in the refrigerator for further study, named as R-CDs.

In order to study selective detection of B-CDs on cations, 12 common cations (NH4 +, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+) were selected for the detection experiments. 0.5 mL of the cation solution was mixed with 4.5 mL of B-CDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were measured and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were recorded. Interference experiments were done to further demonstrate the selective detection of B-CDs on Co2+.

In order to determine the sensitivity of B-CDs to Co2+ detection, 4.5 mL of B-CDs were mixed with 0.5 mL of Co2+ solutions of different concentrations. The corresponding fluorescence intensity was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer. The limit of detection was calculated as LOD = 3σ/k, “σ” is the standard deviation of the blank sample and “k” is the slope of the standard curve.

The selective and sensitive detection experiments were performed in the same way as for Co2+. Several common anions and cations (Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, and NO2 −) as well as dye malachite green (MG) were chosen as interferents.

Tap water was used as the actual sample to examine the Co2+ content. 4.5 mL of B-CDs solution was mixed with 0.5 mL of tap water containing different concentrations of Co2+, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured. The actual sample assay for MB was the same as for Co2+.

The morphology and size distribution of B-CDs and P-CDs were characterized by TEM (Fig. 1 a, b), which showed that both B-CDs and P-CDs were well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles with uniform size distribution, and the average diameter of B-CDs was 9.0 nm (100B-CDs were measured) and that of P-CDs was 8.5 nm (100P-CDs). The UV and fluorescence properties of B-CDs and P-CDs were characterized (Fig. 1c, d). The optimal excitation wavelength of the B-CDs was 469 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 566 nm. The solution of B-CDs was yellow transparent under natural light, and it showed orange fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of the P-CDs was 612 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 622 nm. The solution of P-CDs was yellow transparent under natural light and showed red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Meanwhile, we can clearly see from the results in Fig. 1c, d that the fluorescence color and emission wavelength of the carbon dots change significantly with different doping. The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and P-CDs under different excitation were characterized (Fig. 2 a, b). The emission peaks of both B-CDs and P-CDs do not change with the change of excitation wavelength proving that both of them are excitation wavelength independent. According to previous reports, these carbon dots are homogeneous in terms of their dimensional size and surface structure [36,37]. In order to analyze the possible elements and functional groups on the surfaces of B-CDs and P-CDs, FT-IR and XPS were examined. The FT-IR results of B-CD are shown in Fig. 2c: the 3351 cm−1 peak is the stretching vibration of N–H and C/B-OH, the 2123 cm−1 peak is caused by the C–H stretching vibration, the 1638 cm−1 peak indicates the presence of C = O, and the 1269 cm−1 peak is attributed to C/B-O. The FT-IR results of P-CD are shown in Fig. 2d: The peak at 3270 cm−1 is the telescopic vibration of N–H, 2119 cm−1 is attributed to C–H, 1635 cm−1 proves the presence of C = O, 1171 cm−1 is caused by C–H/P = O, and 1004 cm−1 is attributed to C–H/P-O. Fig. 3 shows the XPS results of the B-CDs. The four peaks at different positions centered at 284.6 eV, 531.8 eV, 398.9 eV, and 194.4 eV in Fig. 3a are attributed to C1s, O1s, N1s, and B1s, with the percentage of C1s, O1s, N1s, and B1s being 51.89 %, 15.55 %, and 22.82 %, 9.74 % respectively. The three peaks at 284.6 eV, 287 eV and 285.2 eV in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) were attributed to C–C/C–H, C = O and C-N, respectively; the peaks at 531.8 eV and 532.7 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) were attributed to C = O and C-OH; and the peaks (Fig. 3d) at 398.9 and 400 eV in the N1s spectrum confirm the presence of C-N and N–H; the peaks at 192.4 eV and 191.5 eV in the B1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) are attributed to the presence of B-O and B-C, respectively. Fig. 4 shows the XPS results of the P-CDs. Four peaks at different positions centered at 284.6 eV, 532.2 eV, 401.2 eV, and 134.2 eV in Fig. 4a are attributed to C1s, O1s, N1s, and P2p, with the percentage of C1s, O1s, N1s, and P2p being 22.32 %, 54.92 %, and 6.77 %, 14.98 % respectively. The three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV and 286.7 eV in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) are attributed to C–C/C–H, C-N/C-P and C = O, respectively; the two peaks near 401.2 eV and 401.9 eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) are attributed to N-C/N–H, respectively; the peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.2 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) confirm the presence of C-OH and C = O; the two peaks located at 134.2 eV and 133.8 eV are attributed to the presence of P-O and P-C(Fig. 4e), respectively. The XPS results of B-CDs and P-CDs are in good agreement with corresponding FT-IR, and the contents of B and P in XPS also proved the successful doping of B and P during the synthesis process. Figure S1 shows the XRD of our two carbon dots; three peaks were observed at 10°, 21°, and 43° for the XRD of B-CDs (Fig. S1(a)) and at 12°, 28.4°, and 41.6° for the XRD of P-CDs (Fig. S1(b)). The XRD of both carbon dots are very similar to the XRD of car-bon nanopowders described in the literature, the broad peaks also demonstrate the amorphous character of the carbon dots [38]. The B-CDs have a broad peak at 21° and the P-CDs have a broad peak at 28.4°, representing (002) plane of the graphene structure (25°) with slight deviation. The broader peaks [39] and deviation suggest that these two carbon dots are less crystalline [40], which may be due to oxygen-containing functional groups [41]. In order to investigate whether these two carbon dots have good stability, we tested their fluorescence spectra with time. As can be seen from Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs and P-CDs are basically unchanged with time, which indicates that they both have good stability.

B-CDs was applied to the detection of cations. B-CDs have a high selectivity for Co2+, and the results were sown in Fig. 5 a, b. The addition of Co2+ can cause an obvious quenching of the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, and this fluorescence quenching is not affected in the presence of other cations (Fig. 5c). Fig. S3a show that the addition of Co2+ (10−3 M) can cause a significant change in the color of B-CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light. The naked-eye detection of Co2+ is feasible at Co2+concentration of 10−4 M (Fig. S3b).

We further determine the minimum detection concentration of B-CDs for Co2+. Before that we first optimized the detection conditions for Co2+. Fig. S4 (a) shows that the optimal pH for detecting Co2+ is about 7. We can also clearly see that the response time of B-CDs to Co2+ is very short, only in 1 min (Fig. S4 (b)). From Fig. 6 a, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs gradually decreased with the gradual increase of Co2+ concentration. Fig. 6b shows the linear relationship between Co2+ concentration (1 ∼ 30 μM) and (I0-I)/I0. By fitting the above linear relationship, the equation for quantitative detection of Co2+ can be obtained: (I0-I)/I0 = 0.0227C-0.0472, R2 = 0.9918 (where “I0” denotes the fluorescence intensity of blank B-CDs, “I” indicates the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of B-CDs and Co2+; “C” indicates the concentration of Co2+). According to the limit of detection formula LOD = 3σ/k we obtained the detection limit of B-CDs for Co2+ to be 0.1102 μM. We all know that an aqueous solution of Co2+ shows a pink color at high concentrations. To disprove the influence of the pink color of Co2+ on the visual detection, we experimentally investigated the Co2+ solutions and B-CDs-Co2+ solutions at different concentrations. From Fig. S5 we can see that Co2+ does show its own pink color at a concentration of 10−2M. However, when the concentration is lowered to 10−3M or below, the pink color is almost invisible to the naked eye. The linear detection range of B-CDs for Co2+ is from 1 ∼ 30 μM, so there is no influence of the pink color of Co2+ on the detection. Table S1 summarizes the detection limits of some fluorescence analysis methods for detecting Co2+.

Two explanations for Co2+ induced fluorescence quenching in carbon dots have been reported in the literature: the fluorescence internal filtering effect (IFE) [42] and the ion-binding effect [43]. The ion-binding effect is generally attributed to static quenching, which represents the production of new non-fluorescent substances and leads to significant changes in the UV absorption peaks of carbon dots [44,45]. We compared the UV absorption profiles of three solutions: B-CDs blank solution, B-CDs with 4 μM Co2+ and B-CDs with 40 μM Co2+, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 6c, the shapes of the UV absorption profiles of these three solutions remained essentially unchanged and no new absorption peaks appeared, suggesting that the ion-binding effect is not dominant in the quenching process. The internal filtering effect (IFE) usually occurs between the absorber and the fluorophore. Effective IFE occurs when the UV absorption peaks of the analyte overlap with the excitation or emission peaks of the fluorophore, so that the analyte can absorb the excitation or emission light of the fluorophore in the detection system [46].The UV spectra of Co2+ showed a broad absorption peak around 400 ∼ 500 nm. And excitation spectra of B-CDs show a peak at 300 ∼ 500 nm which overlapped precisely with the UV absorption peak of Co2+ (Fig. 6d). Thus, we determined that the fluorescence of B-CDs might be quenched by Co2+ via IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of MB by P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: P-CDs was used for selective detection on MB, with Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO2 −, MG as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after mixing P-CDs with 10−4 M solutions of different substances, only MB had an obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of P-CDs. This result indicates that P-CDs have excellent selectivity for MB. We similarly optimized the detection conditions for MB. The results of Fig. S6a show that pH has basically no effect on the detection of MB under pH 5 ∼ 13. From Fig. S6b we can clearly see that MB can quench the fluorescence of P-CDs completely within 1 min. In order to evaluate the quantitative detection ability of P-CDs to MB, we characterized the fluorescence spectra of P-CDs with gradient concentrations of MB, as shown in Fig. 7c. The fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was gradually decreased with the addition of larger concentration of MB, and the relative fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was linearly correlated with that of the concentration of MB in the range of 0.1 ∼ 10 μM, showing an excellent linear relationship. By fitting the above linear relationship, the equation for quantitative detection of MB concentration can be obtained:

(I0-I)/I0 = 0.0334C + 0.0086, R2 = 0.9945 (where “I0” is the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs, “I” is the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of P-CDs and MB, and “C” is the concentration of MB). The detection limit (0.048 μM) was calculated according to the limit of detection formula LOD = 3σ/k. Detection limits are well below the national standards under the China Environmental Protection Act (CEPA) (natural water < 0.05 mg/L, industrial wastewater < 0.1 mg/L). Table S2: Comparison on MB detection limits.

The fluorescence quenching mechanisms of carbon dots include static quenching [47], dynamic quenching [48], and internal filtration effects [49]. Static quenching is generally caused by reaction between the fluorescent substance and the quencher to produce another non-fluorescent substance, which will lead to a significant change in the UV absorption peak of the fluorescent substance. In order to investigate exact quenching mechanism of MB on P-CDs, we firstly record UV absorption profiles of P-CDs, P-CDs with 6 μM MB and P-CDs with 60 μM MB respectively. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 8 a that the UV absorption profile of P-CDs with MB (no matter high or low concentrations) will show a new absorption peak at 669 nm comparing with that of P-CDs, which indicates static quenching, both within and beyond the detection range of MB. The surface of P-CDs is rich in functional groups, and MB belongs to cationic dyes, so we assumed that MB might react with C = O, –NH2 and other functional groups on the surface of carbon dots to generate non-fluorescent substances leading to fluorescence quenching. In order to further confirm our conjecture, we examined the intensity changes with increasing concentrations of MB at varied temperatures. The linear relationship between the concentration of MB and I0/I at different temperatures in Fig. 8b show that the quenching constant of MB was higher at lower temperatures, i.e., the slope at low temperatures was larger than those at high temperatures verifying static quenching mechanism. We further demonstrated the quenching mechanism by the fluorescence lifetimes of P-CDs in the presence and absence of MB. The results in Fig. 8c show that the fluorescence lifetime curves of P-CDs in the presence and absence of MB are basically unchanged, and the average lifetime is 0.974 ns and 0.971 ns, respectively. Therefore, the MB induced fluorescence quenching in this study is static quenching.

In order to verify the feasibility of B-CDs and P-CDs for the detection of real samples, Co2+ and MB in tap water were tested using B-CDs and P-CDs respectively. The results of Co2+ assay are shown in Table 1 . The average recoveries ranged from 99.00 to 100.50 and the associated standard deviations were lower than 4.86 %. The results of MB determination are shown in Table 2 , with the average recoveries ranging from 98.50 to 100.17 and the associated standard deviations were not higher than 5.33 %. The results of real samples indicate that the Co2+ and MB detection methods proposed in this study are reliable and can be used for the detection of real samples.

Rhodamine B was used as a standard to calculate the QY of B-CDs and P-CDs, according to the following quantum yield formula:
Y u
=
Y s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
×
φ u
φ s
× 100 %

(Yu is the quantum yield of the sample to be tested; Ys is the quantum yield of the reference rhodamine B; Fu is the fluorescence integral area of the sample to be tested; Fs is the fluorescence integral area of the reference rhodamine B; Au is the absorbance of the sample to be tested; As is the absorbance of the reference rhodamine B; φu and φs are refractive indices of the solvent).

QY of B-CDs and P-CDs were 77.21 % and 46.19 %, respectively. In order to test whether the addition of L-lysine in synthesis process can improve the QY of the carbon dots, we synthesized R-CDs from OPD and phosphoric acid by the same method as P-CDs. The relative quantum yield of R-CDs was calculated to be 24.38 %. By comparing the QY of P-CDs and R-CDs we can clearly see that the QY of P-CDs is higher. Figure S7 shows the fluorescence spectra of P-CDs and R-CDs at the same concentration (6.25 × 10−3 mg/mL), which indicating that the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs is higher than that of R-CDs. These results confirm the addition of L-lysine can improve QY.

In summary, we used OPD and L-lysine as precursors, boric acid and phosphoric acid as dopants to obtain orange and red carbon dots doped with B and P. Both B-CDs and P-CDs show high QY. B-CDs can selectively detect Co2+ and P-CDs can sensitively detect methylene blue. These two types of carbon dots have good application prospects for the detection of pollutants in water.

Chunyan Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Writing – original draft. Nan Li: Writing – review & editing. Liang Yang: Writing – review & editing. Lei Liu: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Daohan Zhang: Formal analysis, Methodology.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.].

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123824.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
